Giacomo Ceccarini

Junior Gameplay Programmer

Resume ↓

Currently Master’s Student in Computer Engineering at Sapienza University of Rome. Been playing games since 4, been trying to make games since 9. I enjoy turning ideas into interactive experiences for everyone, and by doing so, i continuously improve my skills through practical small projects

Game Programming

3 3 Months Unity (C#)
Forest Runner (2023) ›

Gameplay Programmer / Game Designer

This project is a first take on mobile game development, made in Unity. I worked on the core gameplay (typical endless runner) and game feel.
1 2 Months Unity (C#), HDRP, IK System
Third-Person Controller with IK & Custom State Machine (2025) ›

Gameplay Programmer

A third-person action prototype in Unity focused on a scalable gameplay architecture. It features a custom state machine system for better state management, and uses Unity's Inverse Kinematics (IK) to dynamically align character interactions with the environment, enhancing realism.
1 1 Months Unreal Engine 5, Control Rig, Blueprints, IK
Procedural Walk Cycle with Inverse Kinematics (2024) ›

Technical Animator / Gameplay Programmer

Bachelor thesis project focused on procedural animation in Unreal Engine 5. Developed a dynamic walk cycle system for a multi-legged creature using Control Rig and Inverse Kinematics, allowing real-time adaptation to uneven terrain and environmental conditions.

Hi, I'm Giacomo Ceccarini

I'm a Computer Engineering graduate from Roma Tre University, currently completing my Master's in Engineering in Computer Science at Sapienza. I'm 24 and I've been playing games since I was a kid — that passion never left, it just became more technical. I focus on gameplay and systems programming, with a particular interest in how games feel to play, not just how they work. But I've never wanted to stop at code alone. I believe games are the ultimate form of art: the only medium that simultaneously engages audio, vision and touch. And if you believe that, you have to care about all of it. So that's what I do. I write code, find my way around Blender, and compose and implement audio — because understanding how all the pieces connect makes me a better programmer, and a better collaborator.
